problem text: You possess two cords, each of which consumes 30 minutes to burn entirely from one end, but they do not necessarily burn at a steady speed. Using as many ignitions as needed, you want to measure out a total duration of 45 minutes. How can you achieve this?
problem solution:
First, label the ropes as Rope A and Rope B. We aim to measure 45 minutes in a rigorous, stepwise manner.

1. At time \(t = 0\), ignite Rope A from both extremities simultaneously. This rope will be entirely spent precisely when 15 minutes have elapsed, because a rope burning from both ends consumes its entire length in half the time that it would burn from just one end.

2. As soon as Rope A finishes burning (at \(t = 15\) minutes), ignite Rope B from a single end. By construction, Rope B will require exactly 30 minutes to burn if lit from just one end (irregular burn rates do not matter for total burn time).

3. Once you see that Rope B has been completely consumed, an additional 30 minutes will have passed since \(t = 15\). The total measured time from the original start at \(t = 0\) is thus \(15 + 30 = 45\) minutes.

Hence, by first measuring 15 minutes with Rope A (burning it from both ends) and then 30 minutes with Rope B (burning from one end), you reliably measure out a total span of 45 minutes regardless of how unevenly each rope might burn internally.
valid answer: A valid concise answer is: Light one rope at both ends (15 minutes), then light the other rope at one end (30 minutes), totaling 45 minutes.
problem hint: Attempt to measure a shorter duration first by lighting a rope at both ends, then use the other rope for the remaining time.
